chapters.title: Energy, not time, is the most important resource
chapters.text:
You probably have friends who seem to work endlessly, hardly sleep, and have not been on vacation in a few years. Perhaps this is all about you. Busy work is addictive, as adrenaline fuels an excited state and even arouses pleasure. At the same time, it is tough for you to stop. Workaholism is a habit; in our world, working overtime is welcomed and considered an indicator of success. But linear busyness — stress, lack of sleep, overeating, and lack of exercise — may lead to all kinds of illness and even early death.

At the same time, it is normal to work actively to expand your energy reserves, but you should have a rest of the same quality. By increasing the amount of energy, you will endure difficulties with minor discomfort and more easily achieve results.

Getting out of your comfort zone is only effective if you give yourself time to recover.

Physical activity is the basis of energy. It supports endurance, efficient work, a longer attention span, and emotion management. Proper nutrition, sleep quality, and breathing are mainly responsible for physical health, while exercise fuels physical energy. A deep and concentrated inhale and a slow, calm exhale is a little meditation and a quick way of self-regulation. When we are anxious or angry, we breathe erratically. So, correct breathing is a good remedy for negative emotions. Food is the body's fuel. It is difficult to concentrate on anything other than a grumbling stomach when you are hungry. Overeating also leads to health problems and excess weight. Good nutrition helps to build up positive energy. You will feel energized throughout the day by including whole grains, proteins, strawberries, pears, and apples in your breakfast. Five or six low-calorie, nutritious meals are optimal for maintaining energy. Consume more calories in the morning and less in the evening; during the day, snacks are ok but do not let them exceed 150 kilocalories.

Proper nutrition is primarily about consuming plenty of water.

It is vital to consume foods with a low glycemic index to ensure an even flow of sugar into the bloodstream to maintain energy. Examples are chicken, cheese, green vegetables, dried apricots, almonds, milk, beans, prunes, and eggs.

chapters.title: What makes you stronger: a few principles of full engagement
chapters.text:
There are several principles for living at full engagement.

Be equally attentive to the four key aspects of life: physical, spiritual, intellectual, and emotional. You cannot rely on just one or a few energy sources — each of them must be in balance for full power. Low or overuse of our capabilities negatively affects our energy. Many people are used to seeing life as a continuous marathon. Imagine a runner covering a long distance: they are tired physically and mentally, and their eyes are dull. Now imagine a sprinter; they are full of energy because they give all the best on the track but pause and live a completely different life outside the stadium. It is crucial to speed up and slow down rhythmically.

When starting a race, it is important to see the finish line.

Don't be afraid of stress. To expand your energy reserves, you must go beyond its expenditure limits. Stress (but only short-term) is a good teacher and coach for your physical and mental well-being. After the active routine, give yourself time to rest, and you will notice that you have become stronger and ready for challenges.

Don't underestimate the importance of daily rituals. We are dependent on our habits and automatically do most of our routines. Therefore, you will need to introduce new practices based on your values ​​into your daily life that will make you better. Let's assume that your strong side is effective employee management and outstanding communicative skills. Likely, you have developed a conversation style that motivates and energizes other people. You can make similar positive rituals for different areas of life.

Ask yourself the following questions (a spreadsheet is a good option for keeping track of your progress): “How often do bad habits — smoking, alcohol, fast food — affect my life?” “Do I exercise regularly?” Lack of a healthy lifestyle affects your stamina, motivation, and concentration. Also, evaluate your relationship with your partner, colleagues, and friends. “Do I feel a mutual exchange?” Finally, analyze your inner state: “How do I feel, and what do I want?” Track and write down your thoughts and feelings every week. After answering, you will start moving from the barriers to full engagement.

Did you know? The Japanese term “karoshi” means “death by overwork.” In Japan, about 10 thousand people die from karoshi every year because they work without days off and breaks.

chapters.title: Pauses, or the secret of being productive without burnout
chapters.text:
Greek philosopher Flavius Philostratus was the first to propose a model of alternating activity and rest in a training manual for athletes. The idea is that recovery time should follow exercise to replenish the body's energy reserves. If the intensity of your workout increases, the recovery period should also increase; otherwise, your results will be low. The constant expenditure of energy can lead to stress, illness, and depression. In addition, excess energy is also a detriment, as it can cause atrophy and weakness. The companies that motivate their employees to rest, rather than push them to overwork, work better.

In the absence of constant intellectual activity, our mental acuity decreases, and emotional depth is lost if we do not maintain communication with other people. Nature itself is rhythmic; sunsets and sunrises and ebbs and flows are the pulse of life; that is why nature seems to be the source of balance and peace.

The rhythm of activity and tranquility underlies harmonious existence.

The modern world celebrates work and constant busyness, while relaxation can be considered laziness and lack of ambition. An America Online study found that 47% of its subscribers take a laptop on vacation, and 26% check their work email. However, the inability to devote time to friends and family is not an indicator of importance and success. These are signals about the failure to manage your energy and, therefore, life.

Good athletes know how to recover quickly, even in short breaks between matches. Physical fatigue often causes anger, loss of concentration, and irritation. Recovery periods are important to your performance in your career and your relationships because love and friendships develop between jobs.

How do successful people avoid burnout by doing a lot of hard work? The president of a communications company, Maggie Wilderotter, said that she relaxes walking around the office and talking to employees. She rests and learns more about the life and desires of her colleagues. The head of an advertising company Karisa Bianchi never works on a plane and tries to read books or listen to music. And the vice president of a furniture company, Bill Norman, photographs landscapes to recuperate and stay focused at the same time. Try to take breaks during your workday to reboot: go outside with a colleague, listen to music, read a few pages of a book, or take a quiet walk.

chapters.title: Strengthening physical energy: a few words about breathing and proper nutrition
chapters.text:
You probably have friends who seem to work endlessly, hardly sleep, and have not been on vacation in a few years. Perhaps this is all about you. Busy work is addictive, as adrenaline fuels an excited state and even arouses pleasure. At the same time, it is tough for you to stop. chapters.title: Sleeping and hobbies as proven methods of improving performance
chapters.text:
Sleep is an essential element of recovery. Chronic sleep deprivation can disrupt heart function and affect mood and overall energy levels. The time of day is also important: people who work night shifts are at risk of heart attacks and heart disease. When you work at night or late, you make more mistakes. Don't ignore sleep — this is your most profound rest period. We recover, heal, and grow in sleep.

When doing sports, pay attention to the benefits of interval training — active exercise that alternates with short rest periods. In this mode, we can handle even heavy loads. Plus, interval training can expand your energy reserves and increase your resistance to stress. Research by Ergonomics magazine has reported that people who exercise show high levels of mental productivity and make 27% fewer mistakes.

Physical energy is just the fuel that fills the body. We are most productive when we experience positive emotions. Emotional intelligence is the ability to manipulate and develop your feelings, like muscles, to achieve high productivity and full power. The key muscles are self-confidence, empathy, communication skills, patience, openness, and pleasure. These emotions are a source of positive and motivating energy, while the negative ones quickly “eat up” our reserves. Therefore, it is difficult for people to work when bosses impose fear or anxiety. Conversely, when employees feel that they are being cared for and their accomplishments are noticed, their productivity increases.

Pleasure is a fundamental mechanism for maintaining long-term performance. Strength and energy grow when you do something that brings you joy and confidence. How much time do you spend on relaxation so you can forget about everyday problems? Think of activities that make you happy. Singing, painting, photographing, gardening, yoga — take time for your hobbies and don't replace them with “fast food for the mind” like watching television.

Any enjoyable activity is worth taking the time to do.

Do not forget about mental energy. It develops with visualization, creating weekly or monthly tasks, and finding a creative approach. By keeping your mental muscles in good shape, you can easily switch between tasks and maintain concentration.

We aren't rewarded for taking regular breaks, or for building a workout into the middle of a day, or for any pattern of the work other than keeping our hands down and grinding away for as long as we can. ~ Jim Loehr, Tony Schwartz

chapters.title: Energy management or how to find your purpose
chapters.text:
The brain and body are strongly connected, and exercise improves mental performance by supplying oxygen and blood to the brain. Also, people who exercise regularly are less at risk of memory problems. According to one study, a group of young people showed significant improvement in passing memory tests after three months of regular jogging. Don't stop learning: a new language or mastering a gadget makes the mind flexible and prevents age-related memory impairment.

Spiritual energy balances caring for others and paying attention to personal interests. Living in harmony with our deepest values ​​also depends on adequate rest and the opportunity to experience pleasure in the activity. For spiritual recovery, you can walk in a park, listen to music, or read — these are simple ways to reconnect with your inner Self. A more complex and long-term spiritual practice is meditation, which calms the mind and brings all energy sources into balance.

Regular reflection on your life, values, ​​and goals is a source of energy and motivation.

Setting a goal is the first step in energy management. But the difficulty lies in the fact that you should create your own meanings and purposes. It can be tricky, but you will notice a surge of energy and excitement once you find it. The goal is the right way towards a life of full potential. Philosopher and writer Joseph Campbell called it “the hero's journey.” We go through trials and fight the dark sides of the soul to become stronger.

If we lack a strong sense of purpose, we cannot hold our ground when we are challenged by life’s inevitable storms. ~ Jim Loehr, Tony Schwartz

Test the power of your goal — answer these questions by scoring from 1 to 10: 1. Do you feel the joy of coming to work in the morning? 2. How would you rate the pleasure of what you do? 3. Do you live according to your core values?

If you scored 27 or more, you probably have a meaningful goal. How to evaluate your purpose:
• It should be chosen by you and not imposed by the outside world.
• The goal should be positive.
• It should benefit you as well as other people.
• The goal should be simultaneously sublime, inspiring, and realizable.

chapters.title: Evaluate your life and create rituals that will make you shine
chapters.text:
Your next step is to analyze life at the moment and face the truth. Think about the gap between your dream life and what you currently have. To understand whether you live with your values, answer a few questions in detail:
• How fully do you realize yourself at work, at home, with friends? What do you do, and how do you fail?
• Assess your sleep, nutrition, and physical activity.
• How clear are your priorities, and how long can you keep your focus on them?
• How much negative energy are you wasting and on what (anger, fear, resentment)?
• How productive are you at investing your energy?

After analyzing the answers, try to find the barriers that stand in your way to actual effectiveness. You can include impatience, irritability, the inability to work in a team, a lack of focus, indecision, self-doubt, and the failure to manage time. These are the habits that drain your productivity. Once you've identified them, you can begin to develop new, positive rituals that will change your life for the better.

It is not enough to define your values ​​— you should follow them every day.

We are the creators of our habits, and positive habits attract positive energy into our lives. Rituals guarantee that we will return to what makes us stronger even under challenging circumstances. You may already have unconscious patterns that make you better, like planning your day. Rituals give us a sense of comfort and provide time for helpful thoughts, pushing us to become better people.

Forming positive rituals is the third step in positive energy management. You can devote 20 seconds to a minute to them between work. They will help you structure your life and feel renewal and recharge. While working, you could:
• Listen to your favorite song.
• Engage in deep and thoughtful breathing, accompanied by pleasant thoughts.
• Walk down the street or go up and down the stairs.

For rituals to be more effective, learn to use them during stress; your body will learn to switch quickly. Also, periodically replace them with new habits. For example, don't do the same exercise repeatedly while at the gym. Diversify your physical activity.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
The inability to handle energy resources is the leading cause of burnout, fatigue, and decreased motivation. As soon as you learn how to use and replenish physical and spiritual energy rationally, you will notice an improvement in all spheres of life. The main thing to remember is that you should not be afraid to take time to recover. You are not a machine, and your physical condition affects the quality of your work. Find a routine to do between work to relax; like read a book, or take a short walk.

Goals allow you to use energy properly, as work without a purpose does not have the necessary motivation for development. Analyze everything you have and if you think you are moving in the right direction, identify what you can improve. If the current state of affairs drains you, start small — keep a diary to write down your goals and an alternate plan.

Form rituals gradually: if you want to play sports, you should not overload yourself with all the workouts at once. Start with a 15-minute walk three times a week and extend the exercise over time. Gradually, you will feel like you are ready to step out of your comfort zone, but even a little success in the early stages is a lot of progress. You can create an action plan with dates to record positive rituals. Determine the expected performance impact and document how the new habit helps you live your values.

Don't be negative about your goals — for example, “I won't be jealous.” Only positive beliefs attract corresponding changes.

Once you start working with all four channels of your energy, you will feel capable of more. Do not be afraid of difficulties — they are inevitable when you go to something significant. Step into your own path of a hero boldly and discover the power of full engagement.

Try this

You can research which goals attract and motivate you:
• What are the three most important lessons life has taught you, and why are they important?
• Think of the person you respect. Describe them.
• What are your best qualities?
• If you had to come up with your epitaph, what one sentence would describe your life?

Answering these questions will help you understand what purpose will make your life more fulfilling.

Read it till the end?
